---
title: "Quiz"
description: "Test your knowledge of Automated Text Recognition with a Kahoot-style multiple-choice quiz."
toc: false
---

Test yourself on all sections of this resource. 12 questions — one at a time, with instant feedback.

```{=html}
<div id="quiz-wrapper">

  <!-- Header: progress + score -->
  <div id="quiz-header">
    <div id="progress-bar-outer">
      <div id="progress-bar-inner" style="width:0%"></div>
    </div>
    <span id="q-counter">1 / 12</span>
    <span id="q-score">Score: 0</span>
  </div>

  <!-- Question display -->
  <div id="question-box">
    <h2 id="question-text">Loading…</h2>
  </div>

  <!-- Answer buttons (2×2 grid) -->
  <div id="answers-grid">
    <button class="answer-btn a" data-idx="0">
      <span class="btn-icon">▲</span>
      <span class="btn-text"></span>
    </button>
    <button class="answer-btn b" data-idx="1">
      <span class="btn-icon">◆</span>
      <span class="btn-text"></span>
    </button>
    <button class="answer-btn c" data-idx="2">
      <span class="btn-icon">●</span>
      <span class="btn-text"></span>
    </button>
    <button class="answer-btn d" data-idx="3">
      <span class="btn-icon">■</span>
      <span class="btn-text"></span>
    </button>
  </div>

  <!-- Feedback panel (hidden until answer selected) -->
  <div id="feedback-box" style="display:none">
    <div id="feedback-icon"></div>
    <p id="feedback-result"></p>
    <p id="feedback-explanation"></p>
    <button id="next-btn">Next →</button>
  </div>

  <!-- Results panel (hidden until quiz ends) -->
  <div id="results-box" style="display:none">
    <h2>Quiz Complete!</h2>
    <div id="results-score-big"></div>
    <p id="results-msg"></p>
    <button id="restart-btn">Try Again</button>
  </div>

</div>

<script>
const QUESTIONS = [
  {
    q: "What does ATR stand for in the context of digitizing historical documents?",
    answers: [
      "Automated Text Recognition",
      "Advanced Transcription Research",
      "Archival Text Retrieval",
      "Automated Token Rendering"
    ],
    correct: 0,
    explanation: "ATR — Automated Text Recognition — is the umbrella term covering both OCR (printed text) and HTR (handwritten text)."
  },
  {
    q: "What does CER stand for, and what does a CER of 0.05 mean?",
    answers: [
      "Character Error Rate — roughly 1 error per 20 characters",
      "Correct Evaluation Ratio — 95% of characters are correct",
      "Character Encoding Rate — Unicode coverage ratio",
      "Contextual Error Rank — rank of the most common error"
    ],
    correct: 0,
    explanation: "CER = (Substitutions + Deletions + Insertions) / Total characters. A CER of 0.05 means 5 errors per 100 characters, or roughly one error per 20 characters."
  },
  {
    q: "Which HTR engine does eScriptorium use under the hood?",
    answers: [
      "Tesseract",
      "PyLaia",
      "Kraken",
      "Calamari"
    ],
    correct: 2,
    explanation: "eScriptorium is a web interface built on top of the Kraken HTR engine, which handles both segmentation and text recognition."
  },
  {
    q: "What is the file format used by Kraken for trained recognition models?",
    answers: [
      ".pkl (Python pickle)",
      ".mlmodel (PyTorch-based)",
      ".h5 (HDF5)",
      ".onnx (Open Neural Network Exchange)"
    ],
    correct: 1,
    explanation: "Kraken saves models as .mlmodel files — a PyTorch-based format that can be imported directly into eScriptorium."
  },
  {
    q: "What transcription policy does CATMuS Medieval use?",
    answers: [
      "Semi-diplomatic with selective abbreviation expansion",
      "Normalized modern spelling for readability",
      "Strictly graphematic with no abbreviation expansion (Unicode NFD)",
      "Fully expanded diplomatic with editorial markup"
    ],
    correct: 2,
    explanation: "CATMuS Medieval is strictly graphematic: it transcribes exactly what is written, without expanding abbreviations, using Unicode NFD normalization."
  },
  {
    q: "What is HTR-United?",
    answers: [
      "A commercial cloud platform for HTR model training",
      "A United Nations initiative for digitizing world archives",
      "A community catalogue and metadata standard for sharing HTR datasets and models",
      "A consortium of European libraries offering shared Transkribus credits"
    ],
    correct: 2,
    explanation: "HTR-United provides the HUMU metadata schema and a searchable catalogue (htr-united.github.io) to make HTR datasets and models findable and citable."
  },
  {
    q: "TrOCR uses which encoder architecture to process the line image?",
    answers: [
      "ResNet CNN",
      "BEiT (Bidirectional Encoder representation from Image Transformers)",
      "LSTM with attention",
      "U-Net"
    ],
    correct: 1,
    explanation: "TrOCR uses BEiT (or DeiT for base models) as its image encoder — a Vision Transformer pre-trained with masked image modeling."
  },
  {
    q: "In which scenario would a Vision Language Model (VLM) most likely outperform a dedicated HTR model?",
    answers: [
      "Large-scale digitization of 50,000 pages with a well-known script",
      "A completely unknown script with no available training data",
      "Fine-grained diplomatic transcription of a single medieval charter",
      "Training a new model with 200 manually annotated pages"
    ],
    correct: 1,
    explanation: "VLMs excel in zero-shot scenarios — when no training data exists for the specific script or hand. Once ~50–200 pages of ground truth are available, fine-tuned dedicated models typically outperform VLMs."
  },
  {
    q: "The OICEN-HTR models for Old Icelandic are all fine-tuned on which base model?",
    answers: [
      "TRIDIS v2",
      "Generic CREMMA 1.0.1",
      "CATMuS Medieval 1.6.0",
      "BiblIA_01"
    ],
    correct: 2,
    explanation: "All OICEN models (AlexS, MB, CodWorm, Combined) use CATMuS Medieval 1.6.0 as their starting base, demonstrating CATMuS's role as a universal transfer base for community fine-tuning."
  },
  {
    q: "What is the SegmOnto ontology used for?",
    answers: [
      "Evaluating CER across different transcription systems",
      "Standardizing vocabulary for labeling layout regions on manuscript pages",
      "Defining Unicode character ranges for historical scripts",
      "Mapping manuscript hands to named scribes"
    ],
    correct: 1,
    explanation: "SegmOnto provides a standardized set of region type labels (MainZone, MarginTextZone, DropCapitalZone, etc.) for annotating manuscript layouts in a consistent, interoperable way."
  },
  {
    q: "Which model family is the recommended choice for medieval Hebrew manuscript recognition in the Kraken/eScriptorium ecosystem?",
    answers: [
      "CATMuS Medieval (it covers all scripts)",
      "TRIDIS v2 (its training includes Aramaic texts)",
      "BiblIA (Ashkenazi_01, Italian_01, Sephardi_01)",
      "MiDRASH Geniza 01 (universal Hebrew model)"
    ],
    correct: 2,
    explanation: "The BiblIA family provides three script-specific models (Ashkenazi, Italian, Sephardi book hands) trained on BnF and Vatican medieval Hebrew manuscripts. MiDRASH Geniza 01 specializes in Geniza fragments specifically."
  },
  {
    q: "What is the approximate crossover point at which fine-tuning a dedicated model becomes preferable to using a VLM zero-shot?",
    answers: [
      "5 annotated lines",
      "10 annotated pages",
      "50–200 annotated pages",
      "10,000 annotated lines"
    ],
    correct: 2,
    explanation: "Research suggests that once approximately 50–200 pages of ground truth are available, a fine-tuned dedicated model (Kraken/TrOCR) typically outperforms VLM zero-shot on historical handwriting."
  }
];

let current = 0;
let score = 0;
let answered = false;

function shuffle(arr) {
  // Fisher-Yates (we only shuffle questions, not answer order, to keep correct idx)
  for (let i = arr.length - 1; i > 0; i--) {
    const j = Math.floor(Math.random() * (i + 1));
    [arr[i], arr[j]] = [arr[j], arr[i]];
  }
  return arr;
}

const questions = shuffle([...QUESTIONS]);

function render() {
  const q = questions[current];
  answered = false;

  // Header
  const pct = (current / questions.length) * 100;
  document.getElementById("progress-bar-inner").style.width = pct + "%";
  document.getElementById("q-counter").textContent = (current + 1) + " / " + questions.length;
  document.getElementById("q-score").textContent = "Score: " + score;

  // Question
  document.getElementById("question-text").textContent = q.q;

  // Answers
  const btns = document.querySelectorAll(".answer-btn");
  btns.forEach((btn, i) => {
    btn.querySelector(".btn-text").textContent = q.answers[i];
    btn.disabled = false;
    btn.classList.remove("correct", "wrong", "correct-reveal");
    btn.style.opacity = "";
  });

  // Hide feedback and results
  document.getElementById("feedback-box").style.display = "none";
  document.getElementById("results-box").style.display = "none";
  document.getElementById("answers-grid").style.display = "";
  document.getElementById("question-box").style.display = "";
  document.getElementById("quiz-header").style.display = "";
}

function showFeedback(selected) {
  const q = questions[current];
  const btns = document.querySelectorAll(".answer-btn");
  btns.forEach(btn => btn.disabled = true);

  const isCorrect = selected === q.correct;
  if (isCorrect) score++;

  btns.forEach((btn, i) => {
    if (i === q.correct) {
      btn.classList.add("correct-reveal");
    } else if (i === selected && !isCorrect) {
      btn.classList.add("wrong");
    }
  });

  document.getElementById("feedback-icon").textContent = isCorrect ? "✅" : "❌";
  document.getElementById("feedback-result").textContent = isCorrect ? "Correct!" : "Not quite.";
  document.getElementById("feedback-result").style.color = isCorrect ? "#0f7d28" : "#ad0023";
  document.getElementById("feedback-explanation").textContent = q.explanation;

  const nextBtn = document.getElementById("next-btn");
  nextBtn.textContent = current < questions.length - 1 ? "Next →" : "See Results";
  document.getElementById("feedback-box").style.display = "";
  document.getElementById("q-score").textContent = "Score: " + score;
}

function showResults() {
  document.getElementById("answers-grid").style.display = "none";
  document.getElementById("question-box").style.display = "none";
  document.getElementById("feedback-box").style.display = "none";
  document.getElementById("quiz-header").style.display = "none";
  document.getElementById("results-box").style.display = "";

  const pct = Math.round((score / questions.length) * 100);
  document.getElementById("results-score-big").textContent = score + " / " + questions.length;

  let msg;
  if (pct === 100) msg = "Perfect score! You are an ATR expert. 🎉";
  else if (pct >= 80) msg = "Excellent — you have a strong grasp of ATR concepts.";
  else if (pct >= 60) msg = "Good job! Review the sections where you were unsure.";
  else if (pct >= 40) msg = "A solid start — revisit the Introduction and Models sections.";
  else msg = "Keep reading — the Introduction is a great place to begin.";

  document.getElementById("results-msg").textContent = msg;
}

// Event listeners
document.querySelectorAll(".answer-btn").forEach(btn => {
  btn.addEventListener("click", () => {
    if (answered) return;
    answered = true;
    showFeedback(parseInt(btn.dataset.idx));
  });
});

document.getElementById("next-btn").addEventListener("click", () => {
  current++;
  if (current < questions.length) {
    render();
  } else {
    showResults();
  }
});

document.getElementById("restart-btn").addEventListener("click", () => {
  current = 0;
  score = 0;
  shuffle(questions);
  render();
});

// Initialize
render();
</script>
```
